title: Queue
description: A classic interview problem: implementing FIFO queue behavior using only two LIFO stacks, with amortized O(1) operations.
objectives: Implement a queue using two stacks
Understand why reversing via a second stack produces FIFO order

sections.text: queue's own topic covered push(), pop(), front(), back(). Here's a classic interview problem: building that same FIFO behavior using only stack — LIFO — as the building block.
Given only stack's push()/pop()/top() operations, how do you produce first-in-first-out behavior, when a single stack naturally gives last-in-first-out?
3. The Trick: Two Stacks — In and Out

sections.title: The core idea
steps: When popping is needed and 'out' is empty, move EVERYTHING from 'in' to 'out'
Moving reverses the order — LIFO of LIFO becomes FIFO

sections.text: Reversing a reversed order restores the original order — that's the entire trick. Popping every element off in and pushing each onto out flips their order once; since in itself already reversed the true arrival order (LIFO), this second reversal cancels it out, leaving out in true FIFO order.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: push(1), push(2), push(3): in = [1,2,3] (3 on top), out = []
pop(): out is empty → transfer: out becomes [3,2,1] (1 on top now — order flipped!), in = []. Pop 1 from out → returns 1
pop(): out = [3,2] (2 on top) → not empty, no transfer needed → returns 2
Result: 1 2 3 — true FIFO order, from two LIFO stacks

sections.text: A single pop() can look expensive — O(n) — if it triggers a full transfer. But recall Monotonic Stack's amortized reasoning: each element is pushed onto in once, moved to out at most once, and popped from out once — across any sequence of operations, that's a bounded total amount of work per element, giving amortized O(1) per operation overall, even though any single call's worst case is O(n).

sections.title: Flipping a stack of plates onto a second stack
sections.text: Stacking plates one at a time (in) naturally puts the last plate placed on top. Flip the entire stack over, one plate at a time, onto a second empty stack (out) — the plate that was on the very bottom of the original pile (the FIRST one placed) ends up on top of the new pile, ready to be taken first. That single flip is what turns last-in-first-out into first-in-first-out.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Transferring elements from in to out on every single pop(), even when out already has elements — this breaks the correct order and wastes work; only transfer when out is empty.
Pushing new elements directly onto out instead of in — new arrivals must go through in first, or they'd jump ahead of older elements already in out.
Assuming every pop() is O(n) — most are O(1); only the ones that trigger a transfer touch every element, and each element is only ever transferred once.
Forgetting this classic problem specifically forbids using an actual queue or deque directly — the entire point is building FIFO behavior from LIFO primitives alone.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How do you implement a queue using two stacks?
questions.answer: Push new elements onto an 'in' stack. When popping and the 'out' stack is empty, move every element from 'in' to 'out' (reversing their order), then pop from 'out'.

questions.question: Why does moving elements from 'in' to 'out' produce FIFO order?
questions.answer: Because 'in' already reverses arrival order (LIFO), and transferring to 'out' reverses it a second time — two reversals cancel out, restoring the original first-in-first-out order.

questions.question: What is the amortized time complexity of pop() in the two-stack queue, and why isn't every call O(n)?
questions.answer: Amortized O(1) — although a single pop() that triggers a transfer touches every element (O(n)), each element is only ever transferred from in to out once across its entire lifetime, spreading that cost evenly over many calls.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A queue can be built from two stacks: push new elements onto an 'in' stack, and when popping while 'out' is empty, move every element from 'in' to 'out', which reverses their order a second time and restores true first-in-first-out order — one reversal from 'in' cancels the other from 'out'. Any single pop() that triggers a transfer costs O(n), but since each element is only ever transferred once across its lifetime, the amortized cost per operation is O(1), the same amortized reasoning used for Monotonic Stack.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Queue (Two Stacks)
sections.text: Two stacks = flip a plate pile onto a second pile. One flip cancels the first stack's reversal, giving true FIFO. Only flip when 'out' is empty — amortized O(1).

sections.title: Number of Recent Calls
sections.problemStatement: Implement RecentCounter: ping(t) adds a new request at time t, and returns how many requests happened in the range [t - 3000, t] (inclusive), in milliseconds.
constraints: ping calls are strictly increasing in t
hints: This is 'how many recent items fall within a sliding time window' — a queue naturally models a FIFO window of recent requests.
Every ping adds to the BACK of the queue. Any request older than t - 3000 needs to be removed from the FRONT — exactly FIFO order.
Since t is strictly increasing, requests naturally leave the window from the front first — no request ever needs to be removed from the middle.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store every ping in a vector, then count matching entries with a full scan on every call.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A queue holds only requests still inside the window — pop from the front while they're too old, then the queue's size is the answer.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) worst case, but bounded by the 3000ms window
